mappings.rationaleAI: Mandela's anti-apartheid activism, 27 years of imprisonment, and presidency (1994–1999) including the Truth and Reconciliation Commission and constitutional transition directly address E.8.b.i on the ANC, antiapartheid movements, and first all-race elections.

Covers Egypt's Wafd-led opposition to British presence, King Farouk's reign, the 1952 revolution, Nasser's rise and the Suez crises (1956), the Six-Day War, Sadat's presidency and assassination (1981), and Mubarak's rule — directly addressing B.2 and B.3.e.

Covers British colonization, economic development under colonial rule, independence in 1960, the Biafra civil war, military dictatorships, and democratic governance from 1999 — directly addressing E.4 on West Africa (British colony Gold Coast/Nigeria, independence movements, and post-independence instability).

Covers Reza Shah's regime (1925–41) including social reforms and Allied invasion, Mohammad Reza Pahlavi's reign, the Mosaddeq premiership and CIA-backed coup (1953), oil nationalization, the 1979 Islamic Revolution under Khomeini, and the Iran–Iraq War — directly addressing all C.1 sub-paths.

Covers the establishment of Israel (1948), Arab-Israeli wars (Suez 1956, Six-Day War 1967, 1973 October War), the Lebanon invasion, Palestinian intifada, Oslo peace process, and the West Bank/Gaza self-government arrangements — directly addressing B.3.c.i, and contextually B.3.c.ii (Jordan/PLO).

The Ottoman Empire's dissolution following WWI is the immediate background to Turkey's War of Independence (1919–23, A.1) and Ataturk's secular reforms (A.2); its former mandate territories in the Arab world also set up B.1's Arab lands under the mandate system.

Covers French colonial annexation, the administration of Algeria, the Algerian War of Independence (1954–62), French evacuation, and the post-independence governments of Ben Bella, Boumedienne, and Bendjedid — directly addressing D.1.a and D.2.d.

Covers Haile Selassie's rise, the Italian conquest (1936), Eritrean federation and union, the Derg military government (1974), abolition of the monarchy, the Soviet-backed Mengistu regime, and Eritrean independence (1993) — directly addressing E.5.a.

Covers the 1964 Zanzibar revolt and union forming Tanzania, Nyerere's introduction of ujamaa socialism, the 1979 invasion of Uganda, economic reforms from the late 1980s, and multiparty politics — directly addressing E.6.i.iii (and contextually E.6.b).

Covers the emergence of the Kingdom under Ibn Sa'ud (1924), oil discoveries and exploitation, governments of Faysal, Khalid, and Fahd, increasing control of oil resources, and participation in the Gulf War (1991) — directly addressing B.4.a and the broader B.4.

Covers the War of Independence (1919–23), Ataturk's one-party secularization and social reforms (A.2), WWII neutrality and postwar alignment (A.3.a), the Democrat era (A.3.b), the 1961 constitution, military coups, and Kurdish separatist conflict (A.3.c).

Covers the Hertzog and Smuts administrations, apartheid under the National Party from 1948, Bantu Homelands, the ANC liberation struggle, Soweto riots, constitutional reforms, and the 1994 first all-race elections — addressing E.8.a.i and E.8.b.i comprehensively.

Covers British East Africa Protectorate, the Mau Mau uprising, independence (1963), Kenyatta's presidency (1964–78), and succession by arap Moi — directly addressing E.6.v and the crises of colonial rule in E.6.i.iii.

Covers the Congolese nationalist movement and independence (1960), Katanga secession and UN intervention, Mobutu's Zairian dictatorship (1965–97), and subsequent civil strife — directly addressing E.7.i.

WWII is structurally significant across the section: Turkey's wartime neutrality (A.3.a), Fascist Italy's Libya and its wartime defeat (D.1.b), the post-WWII division of former German colonies (E.1), shift in colonial policies (E.3), and Eritrea under Italian rule (E.5.a).

The British Empire is the colonial authority across the bulk of E (sub-Saharan Africa), B.1 (mandate system in Iraq, Palestine/Transjordan), and E.6.a (British East Africa), with its post-WWII decline driving the decolonization described in E.3.

France held the Syria and Lebanon mandates (B.1.a), administered Algeria, Tunisia, and Morocco (D.1.a, D.2.b–d), governed French West Africa (E.4.a.i), Madagascar (E.6.c), and French Central African colonies (E.7.a) — making it the principal colonial power across multiple outline sections.

Italy's Fascist colonial rule in Libya (D.1.b), its role in post-WWII Libyan independence (D.2.a), the Italian conquest of Ethiopia (1936, E.5.a), and Somalia as an Italian trust territory (E.6.i.ii) are all explicit in the outline.

The Cold War shaped Turkey's postwar alignment with the Allies (A.3.a), the CIA-backed coup in Iran (C.1.b), Cold War proxy involvement in the Angola civil war (E.8.b.vi), Soviet-backed Derg in Ethiopia (E.5.a), and the overall decline of the colonial system under postwar superpower rivalry (E.3).

WWI directly created the conditions for Turkey's War of Independence (A.1), the League of Nations mandate system that governed Arab lands (B.1), and the post-WWI division of former German colonies in Africa (E.1).

Spain's colonial administration of Western Sahara and the subsequent Polisario revolt, division of Western Sahara between Mauritania and Morocco, and Moroccan takeover of the Mauritanian zone are directly covered in D.2.e.

Turkey's alignment with the Western Allies after WWII and its accession to NATO is part of A.3.a's postwar developments, and Turkey's mixed relations with East and West referenced in A.3.c directly invoke its NATO membership and Cold War positioning.
